volumeTitle: Amphitryon. The Comedy of Asses. The Pot of Gold. The Two Bacchises. The Captives
label: Amphitryon (Section 1.1.391 - Section 1.3.503b)

sections.label: Section 1.1.391
sections.original: Dic si quid vis , non nocebo .
sections.translation: If you wish to say anything , speak ; I ’ ll not hurt you .

sections.label: Section 1.1.391b
sections.original: Tuae fide credo ?
sections.translation: Am I to trust in your word ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.391c
sections.original: Meae .
sections.translation: Yes , in my word .

sections.label: Section 1.1.392
sections.original: Quid si falles ?
sections.translation: What , if you deceive me ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.392b
sections.original: Tum Mercurius Sosiae iratus siet .
sections.translation: Why , then may Mercury be angry with Sosia [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.1.393
sections.original: Animum advorte . nunc licet mihi libere quidvis loqui .
sections.translation: Then give attention : now I ’ m at liberty to say in freedom anything I please . I am Sosia , servant of Amphitryon .

sections.label: Section 1.1.394b
sections.original: Etiam denuo ?
sections.translation: What , again ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.395
sections.original: Pacem feci , foedus feci . vera dico .
sections.translation: I have concluded the peace , ratified the treaty — I speak the truth .

sections.label: Section 1.1.395b
sections.original: Vapula .
sections.translation: Take that , then .

sections.label: Section 1.1.396
sections.original: Vt libet quid tibi libet fac , quoniam pugnis plus vales ;
sections.translation: As you please , and what you please , pray do , since you are the stronger with your fists . But whatever you shall do , still , upon my faith , I really shall not be silent about that .

sections.label: Section 1.1.398
sections.original: Tu me vivos hodie numquam facies quin sim Sosia .
sections.translation: So long as you live , you shall never make me to be any other than Sosia at this moment .

sections.label: Section 1.1.399
sections.original: Certe edepol tu me alienabis numquam quin noster siem ;
sections.translation: I ’ faith , you certainly shall never make me to be any other person than my own self ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.400
sections.original: nec nobis praeter med alius quisquam est servos Sosia .
sections.translation: and besides myself we have no other servant of the name of Sosia — myself , who went hence on the expedition together with Amphitryon .

sections.label: Section 1.1.402
sections.original: Hic homo sanus non est .
sections.translation: This fellow is not in his senses .

sections.label: Section 1.1.402b
sections.original: Quod mihi praedicas vitium , id tibi est .
sections.translation: The malady that you impute to me , you have that same yourself . How , the plague , am I not Sosia , the servant of Amphitryon ? Has not our ship , which brought me , arrived here this night from the Persian port [*] ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.405
sections.original: venit , quae me advexit ? nonne me huc erus misit meus ?
sections.translation: Has not my master sent me here ? Am I not now standing before our house ? Have I not a lantern in my hand ? Am I not talking ? Am I not wide awake ? Has not this fellow been thumping me with his fists ? By my troth [*] , he has been doing so ; for even now , to my pain , my cheeks are tingling . Why , then , do I hesitate ? Or why don ’ t I go in-doors into our house ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.410
sections.original: Quid , domum vostram ?
sections.translation: How — your house ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.410b
sections.original: Ita enim vero .
sections.translation: Indeed it really is so .

sections.label: Section 1.1.410c
sections.original: Quin quae dixisti modo
sections.translation: Why , all that you have been saying just now , you have trumped up ; I surely am Amphitryon ’ s Sosia . For in the night this ship of ours weighed anchor from the Persian port , and where king Pterelas reigned , the city we took by storm , and the legions of the Teleboans in fighting we took by arms ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.415
sections.original: et ipsus Amphitruo optruncavit regem Pterelam in proelio .
sections.translation: and Amphitryon himself cut off the head of king Pterelas in battle .

sections.label: Section 1.1.416
sections.original: Egomet mihi non credo , cum illaec autumare illum audio ;
sections.translation: I do not trust my own self , when I hear him affirm these things ; certainly , he really does relate exactly the things that were done there . ( Aloud .) But how say you ? What spoil from the Teleboans was made a present to Amphitryon ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.419
sections.original: Pterela rex qui potitare solitus est patera aurea .
sections.translation: A golden goblet , from which king Pterelas used to drink .

sections.label: Section 1.1.420
sections.original: Elocutus est . ubi patera nunc est ?
sections.translation: He has said the truth . Where now is this goblet ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.420b
sections.original: Est in cistula ;
sections.translation: ’ Tis in a casket , sealed with the seal of Amphitryon .

sections.label: Section 1.1.421b
sections.original: Signi dic quid est ?
sections.translation: Tell me , what is the seal ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.422
sections.original: Cum quadrigis Sol exoriens . quid me captas , carnufex ?
sections.translation: The Sun rising with his chariot . Why are you on the catch for me , you villain ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.423
sections.original: Argumentis vicit , aliud nomen quaerundum est mihi .
sections.translation: He has overpowered me with his proofs . I must look out for another name . I don ’ t know from whence he witnessed these things . I ’ ll now entrap him finely ;

sections.label: Section 1.1.425
sections.original: nam quod egomet solus feci , nec quisquam alius affuit ,
sections.translation: for what I did alone by myself , and when not another person was present in the tent , that , he certainly will never be able this day to tell me . ( Aloud .) If you are Sosia , when the armies were fighting most vigorously , what were you doing in the tent ? If you tell me that , I ’ m vanquished .

sections.label: Section 1.1.429
sections.original: Cadus erat vini , inde implevi hírneam .
sections.translation: There was a cask of wine ; from it I filled an earthen pot [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.1.429b
sections.original: Ingressust viam .
sections.translation: He has got upon the track .

sections.label: Section 1.1.430
sections.original: Eam ego , ut matre fuerat natum , vini éduxi meri .
sections.translation: That I drew full of pure wine , just as it was born from the mother grape .

sections.label: Section 1.1.431
sections.original: Factum est illud , ut ego illíc vini hirneam ebiberim meri .
sections.translation: It ’ s a wonder if this fellow wasn ’ t lying hid inside of that earthen pot . It is the fact , that there I did drink an earthen pot full of wine .

sections.label: Section 1.1.433
sections.original: Quid nunc ? vincon argumentis , te non esse Sosiam ?
sections.translation: Well — do I now convince you by my proofs that you are not Sosia ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.434
sections.original: Tu negas med esse ?
sections.translation: Do you deny that I am ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.434b
sections.original: Quid ego ni negem , qui egomet siem ?
sections.translation: Why should I not deny it , who am he myself ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.435
sections.original: Per Iovem iuro med esse neque me falsum dicere .
sections.translation: By Jupiter I swear that I am he , and that I do not say false .

sections.label: Section 1.1.436
sections.original: At ego per Mercurium iuro , tibi Iovem non credere ;
sections.translation: But by Mercury , I swear that Jupiter does not believe you ; for I am sure that he will rather credit me without an oath than you with an oath .

sections.label: Section 1.1.438
sections.original: Quis ego sum saltem , si non sum Sosia ? te interrogo .
sections.translation: Who am I , at all events , if I am not Sosia ? I ask you that .

sections.label: Section 1.1.439
sections.original: Vbi ego Sosia nolim esse , tu esto sane Sosia ;
sections.translation: When I choose not to be Sosia , then do you be Sosia ; now , since I am he , you ’ ll get a thrashing , if you are not off hence , you fellow without a name .

sections.label: Section 1.1.441
sections.original: Certe edepol , quom illum contemplo et formam cognosco meam ,
sections.translation: Upon my faith , for sure , when I examine him and recollect my own figure , just in such manner as I am ( I ’ ve often looked in a glass [*] ):, he is exactly like me . He has the broad-brimmed hat and clothing just the same ; he is as like me as I am myself . His leg , foot , stature , shorn head , eyes , nose , even his lips ,

sections.label: Section 1.1.445
sections.original: malae , mentum , barba , collus : totus . quid verbis opust ?
sections.translation: cheeks , chin , beard , neck — the whole of him . What need is there of words ? If his back is marked with scars , than this likeness there is nothing more like . But when I reflect , really , I surely am the same person that I always was . My master I know , I know our I house ; I am quite in my wits and senses . I ’ m not going to I obey this fellow in what he says ; I ’ ll knock at the door .

sections.label: Section 1.1.450
sections.original: Quó agis te ?
sections.translation: Whither are you betaking yourself ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.450b
sections.original: Domum .
sections.translation: Home .

sections.label: Section 1.1.450c
sections.original: Quadrigas si nunc inscendas Iovis
sections.translation: If now you were to ascend the chariot of Jove and fly away from here , then you could hardly be able to escape destruction .

sections.label: Section 1.1.452
sections.original: Nonne erae meae nuntiare quod erus meus iussit licet ?
sections.translation: Mayn ’ t I be allowed to deliver the message to my mistress that my master ordered me to give ?

sections.label: Section 1.1.453
sections.original: Tuae si quid vis nuntiare : hanc nostram adire non sinam .
sections.translation: If you want to deliver any message to your own mistress ; this mistress of mine I shall not allow you to approach . But if you provoke me , you ’ ll be just now taking hence your loins broken .

sections.label: Section 1.1.455
sections.original: Abeo potius . di immortales , obsecro vostram fidem ,
sections.translation: In preference , I ’ ll be off . ( Aside .) Immortal Gods , I do beseech your mercy . Where did I lose myself ? Where have I been transformed ? Where have I parted with my figure ? Or have I left myself behind there , if perchance I have forgotten it ? For really this person has possession of all my figure , such as it formerly was . While living , that is done for me , which no one will ever do for me when dead [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.1.460
sections.original: ibo ad portum atque haec uti sunt facta ero dicam meo ;
sections.translation: I ’ ll go to the harbour , and I ’ ll tell my master these things as they have happened — unless even he as well shall not know me , which may Jupiter grant , so that this day , bald , with shaven crown , I may assume the cap of freedom [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.2.463
sections.original: Bene próspere hoc hodie operis processit mihi :
sections.translation: Well and prosperously has this affair gone on for me ; from the door have I removed the greatest obstacle ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.465
sections.original: patri ut liceret tuto illam amplexarier .
sections.translation: so that it may be allowed my father to embrace her in security . When now he shall have reached his master , Amphitryon there , he will say that the servant Sosia has repulsed himself from the door here ; and then the other will suppose that he is telling him a lie , and will not believe that he has come here as he had ordered him .

sections.label: Section 1.2.470
sections.original: erroris ambo ego illos et dementiae
sections.translation: Both of them and the whole household of Amphitryon I will fill with mistakes and distraction , even until my father shall have had full enjoyment of her whom he loves ; then at last all shall know what has been done . In the end Jupiter shall restore Alcmena

sections.label: Section 1.2.475
sections.original: redigét antiquam coniugi in concordiam .
sections.translation: to the former affection of her husband . For Amphitryon will just now be beginning a quarrel with his wife , and will be accusing her of incontinence ; then will my father change for her this strife into tranquillity . Now , inasmuch as yet I ’ ve said but little about Alcmena ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.480
sections.original: hodie illa pariet filios geminos duos
sections.translation: this day will she bring forth two sons , twins ; the one will be born in the tenth month after he was begotten , the other in the seventh month [*] ; of these the one is the son of Amphitryon [*] , the other of Jupiter . But of the younger son the father is the superior ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.485
sections.original: minor maiori . iamne hoc scitis quid siet ?
sections.translation: of the elder the inferior . ( To the AUDIENCE .) Now do you comprehend this how it is ? But for the sake of the honor [*] of this Alcmena , my father will take care that it shall happen at one birth , so that in one travail she may complete her double pangs , and not be laid under suspicion of unchastity ,

sections.label: Section 1.2.490
sections.original: et clandestina ut celetur consuetio .
sections.translation: and that the clandestine connexion may remain concealed . Although , as I have said just now , Amphitryon shall still know all the matter in the end . What then ? No one surely will impute it to Alcmena as a disgrace ; for it does not seem that a God is acting justly

sections.label: Section 1.2.495
sections.original: suamque ut culpam expetere in mortalem ut sinat .
sections.translation: to permit his own offences and his own faultiness to fall upon a mortal . I ’ ll cut short my talk : the door makes a noise . See , the counterfeit Amphitryon is coming out of doors , and together with him Alcmena , the wife that he has taken the loan of .

sections.label: Section 1.3.499
sections.original: Béne vale , Alcumena , cura rem communem , quod facis ;
sections.translation: Kindly fare you well , Alcmena ; take care , as you are doing , of our common interest ,

sections.label: Section 1.3.500
sections.original: atque inperce quaeso : menses iam tibi esse actos vides .
sections.translation: and pray be sparing of yourself ; you see that now your months are completed . It ’ s necessary for me to go away from here ; but the offspring that shall be born do you bring up [*] .

sections.label: Section 1.3.502
sections.original: Quid istuc est , mi vir , negoti , quod tu tam subito domo
sections.translation: What business is this , my husband , since you thus suddenly leave your home ?

sections.label: Section 1.3.503b
sections.original: Edepol haud quod tui me neque domi distaedeat ;
sections.translation: By my troth , ’ tis not that I am wearied of you or of my home ; but when the chief commander is not with the army ,
